beginner.question: How do you add borders to a table in CSS?
beginner.answer: Use the border property on table, th, and td elements: table, th, td { border: 1px solid #ddd; }. This adds borders around the table and each cell. By default, cells have separate borders with spacing between them. To get a clean single-line border, use border-collapse: collapse on the table.

beginner.question: What does border-collapse: collapse do to a table?
beginner.answer: border-collapse: collapse merges adjacent cell borders into a single border, removing the spacing between cells. This creates a clean, professional table appearance where cells share borders rather than having double borders between them. This is the most common approach for styling data tables.

beginner.question: How do you create zebra-striped rows in a table?
beginner.answer: Use the :nth-child pseudo-class: tr:nth-child(even) { background-color: #f2f2f2; }. This selects every even row and applies a background color. 'even' and 'odd' are keyword alternatives to 2n and 2n+1. For striped columns, use td:nth-child(even) or th:nth-child(even) instead.

beginner.question: How do you add a hover effect on table rows?
beginner.answer: Use tr:hover { background-color: #e8f4f8; }. This highlights the row when the user's mouse hovers over it, improving data readability by allowing users to track across columns. Make sure the hover color works with any existing striped styles — you may need !important or higher specificity if the striping overrides the hover.

beginner.question: How do you make a table take up the full width of its container?
beginner.answer: Set table { width: 100%; }. This makes the table expand to fill its containing element's width. Column widths are then distributed based on content (with table-layout: auto) or equally (with table-layout: fixed). Combine with responsive techniques like overflow-x: auto on a wrapper for small screens.

beginner.question: How do you add padding to table cells?
beginner.answer: Use padding on th and td: th, td { padding: 12px 15px; }. This adds space between cell content and the cell border, improving readability. Consistent cell padding is a hallmark of well-styled tables. Text alignment can also be set on cells: th, td { text-align: left; }.

beginner.question: How do you style table headers differently from data cells?
beginner.answer: Use the <th> element selector: th { background-color: #2d3748; color: white; font-weight: bold; padding: 12px; text-align: left; }. <th> cells are semantically header cells and can be styled separately from <td> data cells. You can also use thead th { ... } to specifically target headers within the table header section.

beginner.question: How do you create a caption for a table?
beginner.answer: Use the <caption> element inside the <table> and style it with CSS: caption { caption-side: top; font-weight: bold; padding: 10px; font-size: 1.2em; }. The caption-side property controls whether the caption appears above (top, default) or below (bottom) the table. The <caption> element provides a semantic title for the table.

beginner.question: What is the difference between table-layout: auto and table-layout: fixed?
beginner.answer: table-layout: auto (default) calculates column widths based on the content of each cell — columns with more content get wider, which can cause slow rendering on large tables. table-layout: fixed uses only the first row's widths (or equal distribution if no widths are set) — this renders faster and gives more predictable column control but may clip overflow content.

beginner.question: How do you set the space between cells in a table?
beginner.answer: Use border-spacing on the table element (only works when border-collapse: separate): table { border-spacing: 4px; }. You can set one value (horizontal and vertical same) or two (horizontal vertical): border-spacing: 4px 8px;. This adds spacing around each cell's border. With border-collapse: collapse, border-spacing is ignored.

intermediate.question: How do you create a responsive table that scrolls horizontally on small screens?
intermediate.answer:
Wrap the table in a container with overflow-x: auto:
...
. On small screens where the table exceeds the viewport width, a horizontal scrollbar appears. You can also use CSS: .table-container { overflow-x: auto; -webkit-overflow-scrolling: touch; }. This preserves table structure on all screen sizes without altering the markup.

intermediate.question: How do you create mobile-friendly tables that transform to card layout on small screens?
intermediate.answer: Use a media query to restructure: @media (max-width: 600px) { table, thead, tbody, th, td, tr { display: block; } thead { position: absolute; top: -9999px; left: -9999px; } tr { margin-bottom: 15px; border: 1px solid #ddd; } td { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; padding: 8px; border-bottom: 1px solid #eee; } td::before { content: attr(data-label); font-weight: bold; } }. Each cell requires a data-label attribute matching the column header.

intermediate.question: What are the semantic table elements (thead, tbody, tfoot) and how do you style them?
intermediate.answer: thead (header section), tbody (body section, one or more), and tfoot (footer section) provide semantic structure. Style them: thead { border-bottom: 2px solid #333; } tbody tr:nth-child(even) { background: #f9f9f9; } tfoot { font-weight: bold; border-top: 2px solid #333; background: #f0f0f0; }. These elements also help with printing (headers/footers repeat across pages) and accessibility.

intermediate.question: How do you apply alternating column colors (not just rows)?
intermediate.answer: Use nth-child on cells: td:nth-child(even), th:nth-child(even) { background-color: #f2f2f2; }. For both row and column striping (checkerboard effect): tr:nth-child(even) td:nth-child(odd) { background: #f2f2f2; } tr:nth-child(even) td:nth-child(even) { background: #e8e8e8; } tr:nth-child(odd) td:nth-child(odd) { background: #fff; } tr:nth-child(odd) td:nth-child(even) { background: #f5f5f5; }.

intermediate.question: How do you control the width of specific table columns?
intermediate.answer: Use the <colgroup> and <col> elements: <colgroup><col class="col-name"><col class="col-price"><col class="col-qty"></colgroup>. Then style in CSS: .col-name { width: 40%; } .col-price { width: 30%; } .col-qty { width: 30%; }. With table-layout: fixed, column widths are strictly enforced. With table-layout: auto, widths are treated as suggestions.

intermediate.question: How do you style empty cells in a table?
intermediate.answer: Use the empty-cells property: table { empty-cells: show; } (default — empty cells show their borders/background) or table { empty-cells: hide; } (borders/background are hidden on empty cells). When hidden, the cell appears as if it doesn't exist visually, though the cell still occupies space in the layout (the column width doesn't collapse).

intermediate.question: How do you align text differently in different table columns?
intermediate.answer: Use column classes or nth-child: td:nth-child(1) { text-align: left; } td:nth-child(2) { text-align: center; } td:nth-child(3) { text-align: right; } td:nth-child(4) { text-align: right; font-family: monospace; }. Common patterns: text left-aligned, numbers right-aligned, actions center-aligned. For number columns, text-align: right with font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums ensures numbers align properly.

intermediate.question: How do you handle long text or URLs in table cells without breaking the layout?
intermediate.answer: Several approaches: (1) td { word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: break-word; } for wrapping; (2) td { white-space: nowrap; overflow: hidden; text-overflow: ellipsis; } for truncation (works best with table-layout: fixed and max-width on columns); (3) Use <colgroup> with percentage widths and table-layout: fixed; (4) td { max-width: 200px; overflow: hidden; text-overflow: ellipsis; } for per-cell truncation.

intermediate.question: How do you sort table columns with CSS-only styling (visual indicator only)?
intermediate.answer: You can add visual sort indicators using attribute selectors or classes: th.sort-asc::after { content: ' ▲'; font-size: 0.7em; } th.sort-desc::after { content: ' ▼'; font-size: 0.7em; }. Visual styling only — actual sorting requires JavaScript. For CSS-only sortable tables (limited), you can use radio button hacks with flexbox ordering, but it's very limited and not recommended for real data tables.

intermediate.question: How does the border-collapse property affect the rendering of border-radius on tables?
intermediate.answer: border-radius on tables only works with border-collapse: separate (the default). When border-collapse: collapse is set, border-radius is ignored because the collapsed border model doesn't support rounded outer corners — the browser renders rectangular corners regardless of border-radius. If you need both collapsed borders and rounded corners, use a wrapper <div> with border-radius and overflow: hidden around the table.

advanced.question: Explain the complete CSS table model including the visual formatting of anonymous table objects.
advanced.answer: The CSS table model creates anonymous table objects when the HTML doesn't match the expected structure. The model requires: table → table-row-group (tbody) → table-row (tr) → table-cell (td/th). When elements with display: table-cell are not inside a table-row, the browser generates anonymous table-row and table objects. These anonymous boxes have default styles and can affect layout. Properties like vertical-align: middle on table-cells rely on this model for proper vertical alignment.

advanced.question: How does the CSS table-layout algorithm determine column widths in the fixed and auto layout models?
advanced.answer: Fixed layout (table-layout: fixed): The table width is determined by the table element's width or the sum of column widths (whichever is greater). Column widths use the first row's widths or <col> widths. If no widths are set, columns are equal width. Content is ignored in width calculation. Auto layout (table-layout: auto): The browser uses a two-pass algorithm. First pass measures minimum content width (longest word). Second pass measures maximum content width (full text without breaks). Column widths are then distributed based on these measurements, available space, and explicit widths. This is slower but more content-adaptive.

advanced.question: How do you implement a fixed-header, scrollable-body table with CSS only?
advanced.answer: Wrap tbody in a scrollable container: table { display: flex; flex-direction: column; } thead { display: table; width: 100%; table-layout: fixed; } tbody { display: block; overflow-y: auto; max-height: 400px; } tbody tr { display: table; width: 100%; table-layout: fixed; } th, td { width: 100%; }. This approach has display: block on tbody to enable scrolling, while thead remains static. Column widths must be explicitly set (via table-layout: fixed) to align headers with body cells.

advanced.question: How do CSS display: table values enable table-like layouts without HTML table markup?
advanced.answer: CSS provides display values that mimic table structure: display: table (like <table>), display: table-row (like <tr>), display: table-cell (like <td>/<th>), display: table-caption, display: table-column, etc. These allow non-table HTML to render with table behavior: .table { display: table; width: 100%; } .row { display: table-row; } .cell { display: table-cell; padding: 10px; }. This was used for layout before flexbox/grid became standard, but for data, semantic HTML tables are still required.

advanced.question: How do CSS Grid and Flexbox compare to table-based layouts for different use cases?
advanced.answer: Tables: Best for tabular data (spreadsheet-like), alignment across rows/columns where content relationship is important. Flexbox: Best for one-dimensional layouts, distributing space along a single axis, centering, equal-height columns not aligned by rows. Grid: Best for two-dimensional layouts where elements align in both rows and columns (like dashboards, card grids). Modern layouts should use Grid/Flexbox for page layout and tables only for tabular data, never for page layout.

advanced.question: How do you handle table accessibility with complex tables that have multi-level headers or merged cells?
advanced.answer: For complex tables: (1) Use scope="col" on <th> for column headers, scope="row" for row headers; (2) Use headers="header-id" on <td> when cells relate to multiple headers; (3) Use id attributes on <th> and headers on <td> to explicitly associate cells; (4) Use <caption> for table title; (5) For merged cells (colspan/rowspan), ensure screen readers can still navigate; (6) Consider using role="grid" for complex data tables; (7) Test with screen readers to verify navigation.

advanced.question: How do you achieve equal-height rows in a table when content varies between cells?
advanced.answer: Tables naturally have equal-height rows within the same tbody — all cells in a row share the same height (the height of the tallest cell). This is built-in table behavior. However, for cross-row equal height, you either need to: (1) Use a single-row sub-structure, or (2) Use CSS Grid which supports equal-height rows natively. To vertically center content within cells: td { vertical-align: middle; }.

advanced.question: How do you create a table that supports both horizontal AND vertical scrolling (frozen headers and first column)?
advanced.answer: This requires a combination of positioning and overflow. The most reliable approach uses two separate tables or position: sticky on headers: thead th { position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 2; } tbody th:first-child { position: sticky; left: 0; top: 0; z-index: 3; }. Combined with a wrapper with overflow: auto, this freezes headers and first column independently. Browser support for multi-axis sticky positioning varies and may require JavaScript fallbacks for full compatibility.

advanced.question: How does the CSS spec define the interaction between border-spacing and background-color on table cells?
advanced.answer: With border-collapse: separate, border-spacing creates gaps between cells. The background color of the table element shows through these gaps (since cells don't fill the full table area). The background of individual cells is painted to the cell's border edge (including any border that may be applied). If the table itself has a background, it fills the area behind the cells including the border-spacing gaps. If the table-row has a background, it fills the row area but NOT the border-spacing gaps.

advanced.question: How do print stylesheets affect table rendering and what are the best practices for printing tables?
advanced.answer: For print: (1) thead repeats at the top of each printed page (automatic behavior for semantic <thead>); (2) page-break-inside: avoid on tr prevents rows from breaking across pages; (3) page-break-before: auto on the table itself; (4) Increase font sizes and cell padding for readability on paper; (5) Remove background colors to save printer ink (use borders instead): @media print { table { background: none !important; } td { background: none !important; border: 1px solid #000; } }; (6) Ensure high-contrast styling for black-and-white printers.
